Finding: 1. International operations are mostly reliable, but not punctual
Evidence: Average reliability ~96.3% while average punctuality ~54%. Several months remain near 95–100% reliability but punctuality falls to 40–60%.
Likely Cause: Flights are generally operating, but delays are occurring within the day-of-operation process. This points to operational inefficiency, turnaround issues, coordination gaps, or external process bottlenecks rather than outright service collapse.
Recommended Actions: Make punctuality a top KPI separate from reliability. Track root causes by station and by route. Set a 90-day punctuality recovery plan focused on departure readiness, turnaround discipline, and delay ownership.

Finding: 2. Magh is the clearest operational trough month
Evidence: Magh shows the lowest flights (252) and lowest passengers (40,854) in the year.
Likely Cause: Could reflect seasonal weakness, temporary fleet constraint, maintenance downtime, demand dip, or schedule reduction during that Nepali month.
Recommended Actions: Review Magh specifically for aircraft availability, route schedule changes, demand pattern, and disruption records. Treat Magh as a baseline stress month for planning.

Finding: 3. Strong recovery occurs after Magh, but commercial efficiency softens later
Evidence: Flights rise sharply from Magh 252 to Falgun 335, Chaitra 357, Baisakh 353, Jestha 373. But seat factor peaks around Falgun/Chaitra (~85%) and then drops to about 74% in Jestha, despite highest flight count.
Likely Cause: Capacity appears to have been added faster than demand in late-year months. Scheduling expansion may not have been matched by booking strength or pricing discipline.
Recommended Actions: Reassess schedule expansion in Baisakh–Jestha. Use route-by-route review before adding frequencies. Protect high-performing months/routes and avoid over-capacity.

Finding: 4. Commercial performance is decent on passenger seat use, but weaker on total payload use
Evidence: Average seat factor ~80.5%, versus average load factor ~67.7%.
Likely Cause: Passenger seat occupancy is reasonably healthy, but total revenue-generating payload performance is weaker. This can indicate underperformance in cargo, mix, or total weight utilization.
Recommended Actions: Separate passenger and cargo performance reviews. Add monthly KPI pack for seat factor, load factor, cargo tons, cargo revenue, pax/flight, freight/flight.

Finding: 5. Bhadra stands out as one of the strongest efficiency months
Evidence: Bhadra has the highest passengers per flight (~183) and highest freight per flight (~1,805 kg/flight). Freight is also highest in absolute terms (500,026 kg).
Likely Cause: Strong demand mix, better route composition, stronger cargo market, or tighter scheduling in that month.
Recommended Actions: Treat Bhadra as a best-case reference month. Compare route mix, pricing, cargo bookings, and aircraft assignment in Bhadra against weaker months like Kartik, Poush, and Magh.

Finding: 6. Freight does not move in lockstep with passengers
Evidence: Passenger peak occurs around Baisakh/Jestha, while freight peaks much earlier in Bhadra. Freight is especially weak in Kartik.
Likely Cause: Cargo has a different seasonal and route demand pattern than passenger demand. It should not be managed as a side effect of passenger scheduling alone.
Recommended Actions: Manage cargo as a distinct commercial stream. Build route/month cargo forecast separately from passenger forecast. Prioritize routes with strong belly-cargo contribution.

Finding: 7. Delay profile is driven more by process/external/support factors than by engineering alone
Evidence: Delay breakdown shows IMM 33%, SUBS 30%, MKT/CUS 14%, while ENG 7% and KTM ATC 7% are smaller shares.
Likely Cause: Delay drivers appear heavily concentrated in airport/process/support-chain causes rather than purely technical maintenance causes.
Recommended Actions: Do not frame punctuality as only a maintenance problem. Create a cross-functional delay task force including station ops, ground handling, airport coordination, commercial/customer service, and engineering.

Finding: 8. Engineering is not dominant in delays, but still material
Likely Cause: Technical issues are not the largest driver in this dataset, but still materially affect schedule performance and may worsen passenger confidence.
Recommended Actions: Keep engineering on the priority list, but allocate improvement effort proportionally. Focus on repeat defects, pre-departure readiness, spares support, and maintenance planning.

Finding: 9. There is likely a schedule-quality issue, not just a volume issue
Evidence: Highest number of flights occurs in Jestha (373), but passengers are not highest there, and seat factor is lower.
Likely Cause: More flying alone is not creating stronger revenue productivity. There may be weak frequency decisions, poor schedule timing, or over-serving lower-demand sectors.
Recommended Actions: Review route-level RASM vs CASM, flight timing, and frequency decisions. Use profitability and booking curves before increasing services.

Finding: 10. Reliability drops sharply in Asar and should be investigated
Evidence: Reliability stays mostly high during the year, then falls to about 74% in Asar, much lower than prior months.
Likely Cause: Possible end-of-year operational disruption, fleet issue, schedule instability, weather impact, or reporting anomaly.
Recommended Actions: Conduct a focused Asar post-mortem. Validate whether this is real, and if so, identify station/route/aircraft-specific causes.

Finding: 12. The international business appears to be the core engine and should be managed with tighter month-by-month discipline
Evidence: Total annual international activity is significant: 3,768 flights, 629,147 passengers, 4.4M+ kg freight.
Likely Cause: International sector is large enough that even modest improvements in punctuality, frequency discipline, and cargo capture could materially improve financial performance.
Recommended Actions: Establish a monthly international review pack by Nepali month with: flight count, passengers, freight, seat factor, load factor, punctuality, reliability, delay reasons, and route contribution.
